sentences.text: Yeah.
So the-- there was a girl.
She must have been, maybe, nine, maybe 10 years of age.
She was in a torn dress.
And she would go each day from courtyard to courtyard in a plaintive voice crying, [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] which means, Jewish children, have mercy on her.
And she would be begging.
And it was like a-- like a-- a clock.
Every day, she would be there at the same time, walking around.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hm.
The-- the second part of the ghetto experience was when they liquidated, so called, small ghetto.
There was a small ghetto and a large ghetto, and they were connected by a bridge going over a street, which was not part of the ghetto, over the Warsaw Street.
And one day, they decided to close the small ghetto.
And then, we all had to-- to leave.
We borrowed, or bought-- I don't know-- one of those hand-pulled-- I don't know the name for it.
You know, like-- like people were selling, well, for instance, fish, you know?
Which reminds me, we ate fish that was this size.
[LAUGHS] You could do-- eat hundreds of them at one time.
Anyhow, we got one of those things on two big wheels, you know, pulled by hand.
We put whatever we could from our belongings.
Obviously, not furniture, but clothing and other things.
And we would pull it.
And we never got into the big ghetto in time before the curfew that day, so we just stopped in one of the squares there.
And of course, people were stealing from whatever you had, so we had to watch it at night.
So my father told me, we are going to keep, you know, watch over it.
And he said, why don't you go first?
And after a couple of hours, I'm going to come in-- we're going to go to sleep right now.
And then, in two hours, I'm going to relieve you.
Well, here I was, standing and watching our belongings in the middle of a dark night.
And start-- shooting started.
And I would say this was one of two days in my life that I have never been as scared as I was on that particular night.
There was another one I'll tell you about later, but that was one of them because the shots were coming closer and closer.
And we were-- I thought maybe we were not really where we were supposed to be, because of the curfew.
There was no house, no--
So nothing happened.
And next day, we got into the large ghetto.
Now, the large ghetto, of course, already was being evacuated by the Germans.
So you just went there and looked for an empty apartment.
We would go there and look for an empty apartment, and that's where we would sleep, because we didn't have a house.
We didn't have an apartment there.
So I went around number of apartments, found one that was very nice, said, let's go and stay here.
So we stayed there in that apartment.
My mother, again, found a place in the large ghetto with an apartment.
My mother was able to-- to go-- I mean, [CLEARS THROAT] she was able to get amazing things done under the circumstances.
I remember, she sold or exchanged her fox fur for two pounds of lard.
And she started melting this lard in a big pot on the stove.
And as she finished it and the lard was melted, she gave me a big jar, glass jar.
She told me to hold it, and she poured this lard into the jar.
The jar broke, and the whole thing went on the floor.
That's instances which is difficult to forget.
I mean, it's a little thing, obviously now, but it was a tragedy at that time.
And we had a little dog.
And we no longer had food for the dog, so my father said, the dog has to be put to sleep.
And I couldn't do that.
So I asked my mother to go to the veterinarian and put him to sleep.
And I asked her to bring him back, and I would bury him in the yard.
And she came back empty-handed.
And I was very angry with her.
And she told me she didn't want me to see the dog.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] She somehow-- well, there were a number of, again, as I told you before, factories there when the Jews were working for the Germans-- uniforms and other things.
I'm not quite sure whether this is the right name, but it occurs to me that one of those places were called [?
Tubberman. ?] And mother found a place for us there.
So we would go in the morning there, pretending that we were working there.
But in actuality, we were sitting on the floor in a big room with 100 other people, waiting all day long, until 5 o'clock, or whatever.
And then we would go home.
And luckily, we-- we would get a little [GERMAN], ID card, which said that we were working for [?
Tubbermans. ?] We were gainfully employed for the Germans.
Well, one day, we were sitting at [?
Tubbermans ?] when there was a [NON-ENGLISH] in one of those places.
The building was filled with SS people.
And I think Werhmacht too.
And they yelled to us to go down on the streets.
It was pouring rain.
And I guess I didn't move fast enough, because one of the soldiers-- and I don't know how I know this, but he was Ukrainian in the Werhmacht, felt that I was going too slow.
So he took the butt of his gun, and he hit me right here over the nose, breaking my nose.
I was bleeding.
And I ran with my mother downstairs and stayed in a line of hundreds of people.
And the Germans started two groups.
One group would go into the train to the Umschlagplatz, and the other group would go back to the factory.
My mother spoke excellent German, and I think that saved us at that moment.
Because as we got to the control people, she had this [GERMAN], and she spoke to them in German, saying that we are working there.
And they motioned us to go back to the factory.
What I remember from that particular moment was a-- a family in front of me-- a father, mother, and two little children.
And the Germans, for some reason, told-- pushed the mother towards the Umschlagplatz row, and the father and the children to the factory.
And the father said, I cannot take care of these children.
And he joined with the mother to Umschlagplatz with those two children.
And I thought this was absolutely horrible on his part, you know, to do this.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then there was a second night-- I don't know why it was always raining-- in which they did get us to the Umschlagplatz.
And I was there.
And trains, wagons were standing right there.
And people were again being separated and going left and right.
And again, my mother spoke German to this guy.
And I don't know what she said, but he motioned us away.
It was the second time we escaped.
But at Umschlagplatz, it was quite a sight.
[SIGHS] I think-- I think I mentioned to you before we started that I saw this film, one I have seen on Umschlagplatz.
It was exactly like it.
The train was waiting, those open wagons.
And we really knew-- you know, there were-- there were still Jews in the ghetto who still didn't believe that there was extermination going on.
I mean, there was complete denial in some people that that's what was happening.
Do you know that the Germans actually asked for volunteers?
And there were volunteers who would go, because they were telling them they were going then to a marvelous arbeitslager.
And they were going to work and get money, and food, and everything, much better than the ghetto.
And they were actually volunteering to go with them.
I have never seen this kind of a denial before or after.
[INAUDIBLE]-- hm?
When was this?
That was in '42.
In May, June of '42 they started liquidating the ghetto.
Well anyhow, for the second time, we escaped those wagons.
People were just herded in, and screams and cryings.
And [SIGHS] it was a terrible sight.
But my mother finally said, look, we escaped twice.
It may not happen the third time.
You have to leave.
You have to escape.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She persuaded-- [DRINKS WATER] she got [NON-ENGLISH] papers.
You will see them later.
She got a birth certificate for me under the name of Stephen Halicki, H-A-L-I-C-K-I, polish name, born in Lida, L-I-D-A, which, at that time, was in the part of Poland occupied by Russia.
So she thought that the Germans would never be able to find that this was false paper.
She gave me that paper, and she told me to leave.
They had a bunch of-- what do you call them?
Groups of-- of Jews that were leaving the ghetto each day under supervision of the Wehrmacht, I think, to do some work outside for them-- digging something, or doing something, you know?
So one day-- it was July, I believe, 12, 1942-- I put on myself several pairs of underwear, several pants, several shirts, several sweaters, several jackets.
I was dying inside.
It was middle of the summer.
She gave me those false papers.
My father told me to go to a friend of his who-- gave me the address out in Warsaw.
And he told me to go to our maid in Checiny, C-H-E-C-I-N-Y, which was in southern Poland where she lived, and maybe hide there.
Natalcia.
N-A-T-A-L-C-I-A.
So I went with this-- oh, so my father [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [EMOTIONAL] [DRINKS WATER] met us at the exit.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [EMOTIONAL] [QUIETLY] I'm sorry.
[DRINKS WATER] [?
So I tell ?] my mother-- [TEARFUL] I'm-- [COUGHS] I'm sorry.
[SIGHS] I will never see him again.
[SOBS] And I left.
[BLOWS NOSE]
I went to those people in Warsaw, who told me that they were too afraid to keep me, so I had to leave.
Oh.
So I went to this group of people.
And on one of the streets, the big boulevard, I-- I-- I-- my mother told me what do.
I was-- I-- I-- I decided to be the last one in the group of people.
And the Germans, luckily-- I don't know how it happened-- they were walking in front, on both sides, but in front of the group.
There was nobody behind.
So one moment, I just run.
I went to this man, and he said he couldn't keep me.
I had some money that mother gave me.
Oh.
She went with me to a shoemaker.
She got someplace a gold sovereign, American gold $20 sovereign.
And the shoemaker took the heel off and put the sovereign inside of my heel and put the heel on top of my shoe, so that I would have some money.
I could sell the sovereign.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So I bought tickets to Checiny where the maid was.
It's a small village.
They don't have train station or anything else.
The closest city is Kielce, K-I-E-L-C-E.
By the way, Kielce was the city where my stepmother's parents lived.
And I visited them many times before the war.
And that's why the maid was nearby, because the maid was first their maid, before she came with my stepmother.
So I arrived in Kielce.
And I walked to [POLISH], which was about 20 kilometers.

And I remember walking there, holding in my hand-- which we'll see later-- the false birth certificate I had, so that it would get a little bit yellow on-- through with the sun shining on it, because it was brand new.
And it was 1926.
How could it be brand new?
So many years.
I wanted it to look a little older.
I put a little dirt on it.
I was holding it.
And OK.
Well, I arrived at Natalcia, my-- the maid, who lived in a one-room hut, really, a hut, with some of her sisters.
And they put me on the-- up, there was an attic there with some straw in it.
And I slept there.
And my mother knew where I was going.
And people started talking.
And Natalcia became very upset.
And she was told that I have to leave.
And they-- they wouldn't-- they wouldn't want to keep me there.
Well, my mother somehow escaped the ghetto too.
I still don't know how she did it.
I really never found out.
But she escaped, and she came to join me.
So two weeks after I escaped, she joined me there.
And when she found out that we had to leave, she said, well, let's walk.
So we started walking back towards Kielce.
And we passed by another small village.
And on the side of the road was a house.
And it said 'For Rent.' So my mother went there, and she rented this little house in the beautiful countryside.
And we stayed there for a while.
We didn't have, really, money, so I decided to take a job with a photographer.
And he told me to go around with this album of colored photographs and try to sell it to the villagers, pictures.
I went for a quite time-- miles and miles around.
Didn't-- didn't sell a single picture.
[LAUGHS] They had other worries than having pictures taken at that time.
And finally, Mother said, look, this is too dangerous.
We cannot stay here.
We have to separate.
We cannot be seen together.
We went back to Kielce.
I found a room with some family that I rented.
And I got a job at Ludwig's [NON-ENGLISH], that was a-- used to be a Polish factory.
Now, it was a German factory where they made-- or repaired German cars for the Front and building wagons for the Front, for the Russian Front.
You know, the war against Russia already started.
And my mother decided to go to Krakow.
And she was going to find a job there.
So we separated.
The life in the factory was very difficult.
I didn't have much-- enough food.
I was hungry.
All I ate, mostly, was this big [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what's [NON-ENGLISH]?
No.
Everything in a soup together, which the factory had at noon time.
I had some accidents.
I was knocked out unconscious by machinery one day there and was bleeding and so on.
But it was difficult work.
It was hard work.
We were building those wagons for the Front.
And then we decided, me and my friend, who were making those wagons, to sabotage them.
So we would build them in such a way that the moment they would get there, they would break apart.
[LAUGHS] We were laughing about it and very proud of what we were doing.
The--
No.
No.
Nobody there was Jewish.
Oh.
Oh.
OK, there was a Kielce ghetto.
And I knew that my stepmother's parents were in the ghetto, but I was too afraid to go there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were Jews from the ghetto in the factory, who were brought in each day to work there.
One day, a young woman came to me and said, don't I know you from someplace?
And I said, no.
And she says, aren't you Stephen [?
Halpron? ?] And I said, [?
Halpron? ?] No, I'm Stephen Halicki.
Where do you get that name?
And she said, well, I thought I saw you visiting friends of mine, the [?
Wertheimers-- ?] those are the parents of my stepmother-- a year ago.
And I said, you must be mistaken.
But I was absolutely-- I was so frightened.
I said, that-- that's it.
That's the end of it.
But either she realized that what was happening, or she really didn't believe it was the right person.
But nothing was-- nothing happened afterwards, OK?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
The most unpleasant-- well, a couple of unpleasant things.
I live-- I lived-- again, I had a room with some co-workers there, his wife, child, and him.
And I would sleep on a bed in the kitchen.
But it wasn't-- it was winter, and the kitchen wasn't heated.
And I only had one thin blanket, so I was really freezing there on the bed.
And I would sort of go in a fetal position and cover myself with that blanket.
And I remember getting in the middle of a night and picking up the pot with cold potatoes and eating the potatoes, because I was hungry.
But they told me that I could earn dinners there.
I could eat-- I could earn meals there, by bringing things to them that they needed.
Well, there was no running water in this house.
You had to go outside.
And there was a-- what do you call it?
Hm?
A pump?
No, not a pump.
It was--
You had to take a-- a bucket and go down and pull it up with water.
Well, one day, as he was putting-- pulling this thing, this stick with this hook on it, he lost it, and it fell down.
So he said, make me another one in the factory.
So I made this stick, and I made this hook.
But in order to get a meal, I had to have both of these together.
And I was hungry that day.
So instead of taking one time-- one thing at a time, which would be much safer, I decided to take both of them.
I had a coat, a winter coat, on me.
And I took the stick on the side of the coat, and I opened it so that the guard in the front wouldn't see this.
But then I forgot that I put the hook under my arm here, and he saw it.
And he was Polish.
And I begged him-- I said, leave me alone.
Leave me alone.
And he did.
But the German with the big dog outside noticed it.
And as I was leaving, he got the dog after me.
And the dog caught me by the back and tore the jacket off.
And then, he called the dog back.
And he slapped my face.
And he took this stick and the-- the thing away from me.
And you know, this stays in your mind.
Because from then on, until today, my motto is, don't try too many things at one time.
[LAUGHS] Really.
Every time something happens like this, I remember this incident.
And I said, I-- I [? back it off-- ?] for instance, if I had to carry two things, I said, no, one thing at a time, because I might drop one of them.
It's going to break, you know?
One thing at a time.
That was also a very traumatic incident, at that time.
Finally, I found another co-worker with his wife, who were much nicer to me.
So I moved to their house.
And-- and they would give me dinner.
But I remember, we had rations.
And we would only get one loaf of bread a week from the bakery.
We had coupons that we had been given.
And as you go to the bakery and pick up this bread, the smell of it was overwhelming.
And by the time I got back to my home, half of the bread was gone.
And the rest of it was for-- for the whole week.
But they did give me dinner after a while there.
And I paid something for the-- for them.
But she was cooking, and so it was much better.
The most important thing at that time, for me, I didn't have books.
There were no bookstores.
There was nobody reading.
I couldn't talk to anybody.
Once a week, they had a movie there.
And this was the highlight of my life.
Once a week, I could go on a Thursday and see a new movie.
It was really a treat.
Mostly German movies with Polish subtitles.
But, you know, to see movies, I mean, something, you know, experience something different.
OK.
I corresponded with my mother, who was in Krakow.
And one day, I decided to visit her.
So I went on a train, and I went to her-- to her place, to her apartment.
I went upstairs.
She was upstairs.
Now, the rooms were rented in the apartment by people.
There were a number of rooms.
But there were some rooms which had a room behind.
And you had to go through the room in the middle to go outside.
My mother had the middle room.
And unlucky enough, the room behind her was occupied by a [? volksdeutsche. ?] You know what a [? volksdeutsche ?] is?
OK.
But you should explain it anyway.
Somebody who-- [INAUDIBLE] found out that the grandfather was German.
And therefore, they had special things from the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was with my mother, who had false papers.
Her name was Anka.
The false name was Anka, her first name.
I even forgot, although I have it in the paper someplace, her last name.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Doorbell.
Comes in a burly SS officer with a Jewish policeman.
They had Jewish policemen.
He had a jacket on.
And he picked up their lapel on his jacket, and underneath was a-- the cross bones and-- and sign of Gestapo.
And says-- can I say it in German?
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Do you know what it is?
Gestapo.
He put handcuffs on my mother and me, and my mother-- and said, let's go.

And my mother, [EMOTIONAL] thinking about me always, said, could we put a sweater on our hands with the handcuffs, so that my son wouldn't be embarrassed walking down the street with a handcuff on him?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
He took us to this German prison.
And there was a-- a-- a yard.
There was a-- a wire fence.
Behind the wire fence was about six or seven single rooms on the first floor, with a little window on the top. .
No toilet.
There was a bucket for the-- as a toilet.
And I showed him my papers.
Oh.
